claim: For all integers n > 1, n is prime if and only if (n-1)! + 1 is divisible by n.
For any prime n, the factorial (n-1)! is congruent to -1 modulo n.
If n > 4 is composite, then (n-1)! is divisible by n.
For n > 1, the greatest common divisor of (n-1)! and n is 1 if n is prime, and n if n > 4 is composite.
predicate: (n <= 4) or (is_prime(n) and gcd(factorial(n-1), n) == 1) or (not is_prime(n) and gcd(factorial(n-1), n) == n)

claim: If n is prime, then n divides (n-1)! + 1 and therefore gcd((n-1)!, n) = 1.
If n is prime, then for all 1 <= k < n, k is invertible modulo n.
predicate: (not is_prime(n)) or all(gcd(k, n) == 1 for k in range(1, n))

claim: If n is prime, (n-2)! is congruent to 1 modulo n.
For all integers n > 1, phi(n) = n - 1 if and only if n is prime.
If n is prime, then for any a not divisible by n, a^(n-1) is 1 modulo n.
predicate: (not is_prime(n)) or all(pow(a, n-1, n) == 1 for a in range(1, min(n, 10)) if gcd(a, n) == 1)

claim: If n is prime, n divides 2^n - 2.
If n is prime, then n divides (n-1)! + 2^n - 1.
predicate: (not is_prime(n)) or ((factorial(n-1) + pow(2, n, n*n) - 1) % n == 0)

claim: For all integers n > 1, sigma(n) = n + 1 if and only if n is prime.
For n > 1, n is prime if and only if sigma(n) + phi(n) = 2n.
If n is prime, then n and sigma(n) are relatively prime.
If n is prime, then phi(sigma(n)) is even for n > 2.
If n is prime, then (n-3)! is congruent to (n-1)/2 modulo n for odd n.
If n is prime, then n divides (n-1)! + phi(n) + 1.
If n is prime, then n divides 2^n + phi(n) - 1.
predicate: (not is_prime(n)) or ((pow(2, n, n*n) + phi(n) - 1) % n == 0)

claim: For any prime p, the exponent of p in p! is exactly 1.
For any prime p, the exponent of p in (p^2)! is p + 1.
If p is prime and p >= 5, then (p-1)! + 1 is divisible by p^2 if and only if p is a Wilson prime.
If n is prime, the number of divisors of n is exactly 2.
For n > 1, n is prime if and only if sigma(n) + phi(n) = n * tau(n).
If n is prime, every binomial coefficient C(n, k) for 0 < k < n is divisible by n.
predicate: (not is_prime(n)) or all((factorial(n)//(factorial(k)*factorial(n-k))) % n == 0 for k in range(1, n))

claim: If n is prime, (1 + x)^n is congruent to 1 + x^n modulo n.
predicate: (not is_prime(n)) or (pow(1 + 2, n, n) == (1 + pow(2, n, n)) % n)

claim: If n is prime, then n divides (2^n - 2), which is consistent with the expansion of (1+1)^n.
If n is an odd prime, ((n-1)/2)! squared is congruent to -(-1)^((n-1)/2) modulo n.
predicate: (not (is_prime(n) and n % 2 == 1)) or (pow(factorial((n-1)//2), 2, n) == (n - pow(-1, (n-1)//2, n)) % n)

claim: If n is a prime of the form 4k + 1, then -1 is a quadratic residue modulo n.
predicate: (not (is_prime(n) and n % 4 == 1)) or any(pow(x, 2, n) == n - 1 for x in range(1, n))

claim: If n is a prime of the form 4k + 3, then ((n-1)/2)! is congruent to +/- 1 modulo n.
For any prime p, sigma(p^k) = (p^(k+1) - 1)/(p - 1).
For prime p, sigma(p^2) is always an odd integer.
For prime p, phi(p^k) = p^k - p^(k-1).
For any prime p, gcd(sigma(p^2), phi(p^2)) = 1.
If n is prime, then n divides (n-1)! + sigma(n) - n.
If n is prime, the product of its divisors is n^(tau(n)/2).
If n is prime, then a^(phi(n)) is congruent to 1 modulo n for gcd(a,n)=1.
predicate: (not is_prime(n)) or all(pow(a, phi(n), n) == 1 for a in range(1, min(n, 5)) if gcd(a, n) == 1)

claim: If p is prime, the sum of k^(p-1) for 1 <= k < p is congruent to -1 modulo p.
predicate: (not is_prime(n)) or (sum(pow(k, n-1, n) for k in range(1, n)) % n == n - 1)

claim: If p is prime, (p-1)! is congruent to the sum of k^(p-1) modulo p.
predicate: (not is_prime(n)) or (factorial(n-1) % n == sum(pow(k, n-1, n) for k in range(1, n)) % n)

claim: An integer n is the sum of two squares if and only if every prime factor p = 4k+3 has an even exponent in n.
predicate: (n < 0) or ((not any(vp(n, p) % 2 != 0 for p in range(3, n+1, 4) if is_prime(p))) == any(x*x + y*y == n for x in range(int(n**0.5)+1) for y in range(int(n**0.5)+1)))

claim: Any prime p = 4k+1 is a sum of two squares.
predicate: (not (is_prime(n) and n % 4 == 1)) or any(x*x + y*y == n for x in range(int(n**0.5)+1) for y in range(int(n**0.5)+1))

claim: No prime p = 4k+3 is a sum of two squares.
predicate: (not (is_prime(n) and n % 4 == 3)) or not any(x*x + y*y == n for x in range(int(n**0.5)+1) for y in range(int(n**0.5)+1))

claim: For prime p, the sum of divisors of p^k is odd if and only if p=2 or k is even.
sigma(n) is odd if and only if the odd part of n is a perfect square.
For any prime p > 2, sigma(p) is even because its odd part (p) is not a perfect square.
For prime p, n divides the power sum sum_{k=1}^{p-1} k^m if (p-1) does not divide m.
predicate: (not is_prime(n)) or (n == 2) or (sum(pow(k, 2, n) for k in range(1, n)) % n == 0)

claim: If n is prime, sigma(n) and phi(n) have the same parity for n > 2.
If n > 4 is composite, then n divides (n-1)! and thus gcd(n, (n-1)!+1) = 1.
For n > 1, n is prime if and only if gcd(n, (n-1)! + 1) = n.
For prime p, tau(p^k) = k + 1.
For prime p, gcd(sigma(p^k), phi(p^k)) is always a power of p.
